notes: Foundation trick. Master this before all others. Short 5-min sessions work best.

methods.steps: 1. Hold treat above dog's nose. 2. Slowly move treat back over head. 3. Dog's bottom naturally lowers. 4. Say 'Sit' as bottom touches ground. 5. Reward immediately. 6. Repeat 5-10x per session.

notes: Critical for safety. Proof stay in many environments before relying on it.

methods.steps: 1. Ask for Sit. 2. Say 'Stay', wait 2 seconds, reward. 3. Increase duration before adding distance. 4. Add 1 step back, return, reward. 5. Never add distraction until duration+distance solid.

notes: Most important safety skill. Never punish a dog for coming, even if late. Use highest value rewards.

methods.best_for: Young dogs, social breeds (Goldens, Labs, Cavaliers)
methods.steps: 1. Crouch down, open arms, enthusiastic voice. 2. Say 'Come!' once clearly. 3. When dog arrives, celebrate hugely. 4. Never call dog for anything unpleasant. 5. Always make coming to you the BEST thing.

notes: Down is more submissive than sit - some dominant dogs resist. Take it slow. Never force.

methods.steps: 1. Dog in Sit. 2. Hold treat to nose, move straight down to floor. 3. Slide treat away from dog along floor. 4. Dog follows into down. 5. Say 'Down' as elbows touch floor. 6. Reward. Never push dog down.

notes: Extend to 'Other Paw' as a second trick. Great for socialization and vet visits.

methods.steps: 1. Ask for Sit. 2. Hold treat in closed fist, present at paw height. 3. Dog paws at fist. 4. Open hand and reward. 5. Add 'Shake' cue. 6. Transition to offering open hand.

notes: Teach 'left' and 'right' directions. Good warm-up exercise.

methods.steps: 1. Hold treat at dog's nose. 2. Slowly move treat in a circle (left or right). 3. Dog follows nose. 4. Complete full circle. 5. Reward. 6. Add 'Spin' or 'Twirl' cue. 7. Fade lure - use hand signal.

notes: Some dogs dislike exposing belly - respect this. Large breeds may need more patience.

methods.steps: 1. Dog in Down. 2. Hold treat at nose. 3. Move treat toward dog's shoulder (not over head). 4. Dog follows treat, begins to roll. 5. Continue arc to complete roll. 6. Mark and reward. Break into stages if needed.

notes: Crowd favorite! Teach distinct 'release' cue so dog knows when to get up.

methods.steps: 1. Dog in Down. 2. Lure nose toward dog's hip to cause side tilt. 3. Dog rolls to side. 4. Mark moment dog is fully on side, still. 5. Reward stillness. 6. Add 'Bang!' cue + finger gun gesture.

notes: Not all breeds retrieve naturally. For scent hounds and toy breeds, require extra motivation.

methods.steps: 1. Roll/toss toy short distance. 2. Dog chases. 3. Run away from dog as it returns (builds chase instinct). 4. When dog brings toy, trade for treat. 5. Gradually throw farther.

notes: Safety skill. Proof with food on ground, other dogs, dropped items, wildlife.

methods.steps: 1. Put low-value treat in closed fist. 2. Present fist to dog. 3. When dog stops sniffing/pawing, open hand. 4. Do NOT give fist treat - give treat from other hand. 5. Add 'Leave It' cue.

notes: Consistency is key - every walk is a training opportunity. Use high rate of reinforcement initially.

methods.steps: 1. Dog pulls, you STOP. 2. Wait for leash to slacken. 3. Resume walking. 4. Repeat consistently. 5. Dog learns pulling = stopping, loose leash = forward movement.

notes: Foundation for all distracting environments. Prerequisite for heel and recall proofing.

methods.steps: 1. Hold treat between your eyes. 2. Wait for dog to look at your eyes. 3. Mark and reward. 4. Add 'Watch Me' cue. 5. Build duration. 6. Proof in distracting environments.

notes: Useful for keeping dog settled during meals, guests, etc. Maintain with random rewards.

methods.steps: 1. Place mat on floor. 2. Lure dog onto mat. 3. Reward all four paws on mat. 4. Ask for Down on mat. 5. Reward on mat, release with 'OK'. 6. Gradually increase distance from mat.

notes: Never force jump height. Joint health first. Low/no jumping for puppies under 1 year.

methods.steps: 1. Hold hoop on ground (walk through). 2. Lure dog through with treat. 3. Reward. 4. Gradually raise hoop. 5. Add jump height slowly. 6. Add 'Jump' cue once dog is enthusiastic.

notes: Fun agility precursor. Small dogs often find this easier. Use low body position cue.

methods.steps: 1. Stand with legs apart. 2. Lure treat through one leg. 3. Lure back through other leg. 4. Reward after figure-8. 5. Add 'Weave' cue. 6. Build speed with enthusiasm.

notes: ALWAYS teach 'Quiet' alongside 'Speak'. Never reward nuisance barking.

methods.steps: 1. Wait for dog to naturally bark. 2. Mark immediately + reward. 3. Add 'Speak' just before dog barks. 4. Dog learns cue predicts bark = reward. 5. Always pair with 'Quiet' training.

notes: Address underlying cause (anxiety, boredom, territory). Exercise and enrichment help.

methods.steps: 1. Dog barks. 2. Say 'Quiet' calmly once. 3. Wait silently. 4. The moment there is even 1 second of quiet, reward. 5. Gradually require longer quiet periods before reward.

notes: Useful for practical situations (door manners, tight spaces). Combine with 'Wait'.

methods.steps: 1. Dog stands facing you. 2. Walk slowly toward dog. 3. Dog steps back. 4. Mark and reward. 5. Add 'Back' cue as dog moves backward. 6. Fade body movement.

notes: Fun crowd pleaser. Teach 'Wave' as extension (paw without touching hand).

methods.steps: 1. Ask for Shake. 2. Gradually raise your hand higher. 3. Dog raises paw to match. 4. When paw meets raised hand, reward. 5. Add 'High Five' cue.

notes: Adorable trick for photos and greetings. Teach separately from High Five with different cue.

methods.steps: 1. Present hand for High Five slightly out of reach. 2. Dog waves paw trying to reach. 3. Mark and reward the wave. 4. Gradually increase distance. 5. Add 'Wave' cue.

notes: Natural behavior for many dogs. Great trick to end a performance sequence.

methods.steps: 1. Dog standing. 2. Hold treat at nose, lure straight down to floor between front paws. 3. Dog elbows lower while hips stay up. 4. Mark and reward. 5. Add 'Bow' cue. 6. If dog downs, lure more forward.

notes: Foundation of all training. Name = 'pay attention to me'. Never punish dog after calling name.

methods.steps: 1. Say dog's name once, cheerfully. 2. When dog looks at you, reward immediately. 3. Practice in quiet environment first. 4. Never repeat name multiple times. 5. Name should always predict good things.

notes: Clean accidents with enzyme cleaner. Never punish accidents - only reward success.

methods.steps: 1. Take dog to potty spot. 2. Wait. 3. Say 'Go Potty' as dog begins to eliminate. 4. Reward immediately after finishing. 5. Consistency: same spot, same cue, same reward every time.

notes: Excellent mental enrichment. Tires dogs out as much as physical exercise. Great for anxious dogs.

methods.steps: 1. Scatter kibble on grass. 2. Say 'Find It!' 3. Dog searches with nose. 4. All food is reward. 5. Progress to hiding food in boxes/containers.

notes: Requires impulse control. Long-nosed breeds easier. Fun parlor trick showing self-control.

methods.best_for: Patient, calm breeds (Basset, Saint Bernard, Newfoundland)
methods.steps: 1. Master extended Stay first. 2. Place treat on flat nose. 3. Say 'Stay'. 4. Wait 1 second, say 'OK', dog flips treat. 5. Gradually increase duration.

notes: Advanced trick requiring body awareness. Use proper skateboard size for dog. Safety first.

methods.best_for: Confident, bold breeds (Bull Terrier, Boxer, Aussie)
methods.steps: 1. Reward stepping on stationary board. 2. Rock board slightly, reward calm. 3. All four feet on board. 4. Lure movement. 5. Celebrate any rolling.

notes: Practical skill for service dogs. Requires physical strength appropriate for door weight.

methods.steps: 1. Teach nose/paw target first. 2. Transfer target to door surface. 3. Reward touching door. 4. Reward pushing door. 5. Shape full push open. 6. Add 'Open' cue.

notes: Retrievers and spaniels excel. Useful for fetching mail, carrying own leash, service tasks.

methods.steps: 1. Teach 'Take It' (hold item in mouth). 2. Teach 'Hold' (keep holding). 3. 'Give' or 'Drop'. 4. Combine: Take-Hold-Walk-Give. 5. Add 'Carry' for walking with item.

notes: Complex behavior chain. High cognitive load. Teach each component separately first.

methods.method_name: Chain: Pick Up + Drop In Box
methods.steps: 1. Teach 'Drop It' into container. 2. Start with toy right at box edge. 3. Dog picks up toy, drops in box. 4. Reward jackpot. 5. Increase distance from box. 6. Add 'Tidy Up' cue.

notes: Very few dogs achieve this. Border Collies (like Chaser with 1000+ words) are exceptional. Requires months of consistent training.

methods.best_for: Very intelligent breeds only (Border Collie, Poodle)
methods.steps: 1. Introduce one object (e.g., 'ball'). 2. Say 'Get ball', dog gets it, huge reward. 3. Add second object after 50+ correct reps. 4. Mix objects. 5. Dog must discriminate. 6. Build vocabulary gradually.

notes: Always check with vet to confirm dog has no actual lameness. Very impressive performance trick.

methods.best_for: Coordinated breeds with solid paw tricks
methods.steps: 1. Teach dog to hold paw up in Sit. 2. While dog holds paw up, ask for forward movement. 3. Dog hops forward on 3 legs. 4. Mark and reward. 5. Build distance. 6. Add 'Limp' cue.

notes: Great body awareness exercise. Check that surface is comfortable for dog.

methods.steps: 1. Dog in Down. 2. Hold treat at nose level on ground. 3. Move treat forward slowly. 4. Dog crawls forward to follow. 5. Mark movement, reward. 6. Add 'Crawl' cue. 7. Reward only crawl not stand.
